title: A Stab at RL for Trading
text:
General-sum Multi-agent RL (MARL)
Source: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning: A Selective Overview of Theories and Algorithms (2021)
Remember, we are not trying to model the entire market dynamics, but rather trying to find theories and models that not only better explain the dynamics but are capable to produce better returns than existing solutions. General-sum MARL should inject exciting new insights to develop or apply better algorithms in our case. We will not look into cooperative MARL (unbounded sum) nor competitive MARL (zero/constant-sum) per the following note.






NoteMisconception: Zero-sum environment does not mean zero-sum games





Suppose \(R_1,R_2,\cdots,R_n\) represents each of the \(n\) agents’ rewards.
A competitive \(n=2\) game is trivially formulated as \(R_1+R_2=0 \implies R_1=-R_2\). Optimizing in this game just results in maximizing \(R_1\) and minimizing \(R_2\) (i.e., maximizing \(-R_2\)). 2 players competing for a limited resource/reward/return/utility (i.e., zero-sum/constant-sum).
\[
\max_{\pi_1} \mathbb{E}[R_1], \max_{\pi_2} \mathbb{E}[R_2] \implies \max_{\pi_1}\min_{\pi_2}\mathbb{E}[R]
\]
Remember, we assume all \(n\) players have a shared reward structure.
What about competitive \(n>2\) game? These are commonly modelled as zero-sum games. While an \(n\)-player poker game and \(n\)-traders trading at an exchange are all examples of zero-sum environment, we will quickly see how it starts to break down game-theoretically speaking. Let us consider \(n=3\). We have \(R_1+R_2+R_3=0 \implies R_1=-R_2-R_3, R_2=-R_1-R_3, R_3=-R_1-R_2\). If all three are to be simultaneously maximized, how do we optimize it?
Like this? \[
\max_{\pi_1}\min_{\pi_2}\text{maxmin??}_{\pi_3}\mathbb{E}[R]
\]
It quickly devolves into a mess with unclear optimization goals and proven to be PPAD-complete.
So what does it mean to be in a fully competitive setting and how does it diverge to what zero-sum environment mean? Zero-sum environment just means zero-sum, but it does not limit the formation of coalition, which contradicts what it means for a game to be fully competitive. A coalition naturally occurs even for 3-players, where 1-player wins and 2-player naturally form a group to share the losses, either inadverdently or intentionally. While there’s probably more to look into \(n>2\) fully competitive game, we will focus only on large \(n\) mixed setting MARL, where it is neither fully cooperative nor fully competitive (e.g., teams).




Markov games (MG)
a.k.a. Stochastic games
In direct generalization of MDP, Markov games are defined as \((\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{S}, \{\mathcal{A}^i\}, \mathcal{P}, \{\mathcal{R}^i\}, \gamma)\) where each \(i\)th agent of \(\mathcal{N}\) has their own actions and their own reward functions while all part of the same environment (i.e., state space). For every \(i\)th agent given a joint policy \(\pi(a|s):=\prod \pi^i(a^i|s)\), we simulatenously obtain each agent’s value.
\[
V^i_{\pi^i,\pi^{-i}}(s) := \mathbb{E}\bigg[\sum_{t\geq 0} \gamma^t R^i \bigg| a_t^i \sim \pi^i(\cdot|s_t), s_0 = s \bigg]
\]
where \(-i\) is all the agents in \(\mathcal{N}\) except the \(i\)th agent.
A big difference between MDP is now the optimal performance of the \(i\)th agent has to also include the choices made by other agents \(-i\) in its environment.
Now, why Markov Games? Because it (and other game models) allows us to define solutions we are interested in and analyze various properties of MARL (e.g., existence of convergence, uniqueness of convergence, convergence complexity, etc…). In this case, a popular solution concept we are interested in is Nash equilibrium (NE). For all agents \(i\),
\[
V^i_{\pi^{i,*},\pi^{-i,*}}(s) \geq V^i_{\pi^i, \pi^{-i,*}}(s)
\]
Under NE at equilibrium point of \(\pi^*\), for each agent \(i\in\mathcal{N}\) and all of it’s states \(s\in\mathcal{S}\), any deviation in its policy (\(\pi^i\)) will result in smaller value for any states for all agents assuming \(\pi^{{-i},*}\) is optimal. In other words, all of the agents has no incentive to deviate their policy. This means in-production, each agent will follow its own NE-optimal policy that tries to maximize profits and minimize losses fully knowing other agents are also trying to maximize profits and minimize losses against them.
For the sake of completion, Normal-form games (NFGs) is another well-known kind of game within MG to work with simpler models. Notably, 2-player NFGs can be represented as matrix and solved via linear algebra (e.g., linear programming) to find the Nash equilibrium.


Extensive-form games (EFG)
We will not delve into EFG too thoroughly since not much theoretical research has been done on EFG for general-sum setting (let alone MARL). (I don’t know why? And lowkey this is already on the edge of my interest.) Partially observerable Markov/Stochastic game (POSG) has been proposed, but it is even theoretically proven to be strictly more intractable, while EFG at least admits polynomial-time convergence (class P) under “mild conditions.” Yes, EFG is a way to model partial observations (e.g., poker) in game theoretic way, but it seems POSG is more popular in-relation to MARL for some various reasons. (Perhaps its because EFG’s restriction to finite state space, which means the game model must be discrete.)
Tangent: In RL, and especially MARL, we have talked about complexity and convergence issues all the time, but why (un)supervised ML are not plagued with these issues? Recall, when there are multiple agents training against/with each other, it creates a moving target in the environment that causes the gradient-based optimization to take a longer time to reach a solution. Sometimes, there might not even be a fixed-point convergence or it reaches a state where the gradients loops back each other (i.e., intractable and vague description of solutions). This is just one kind of problem and MARL is plagued with many other problems. Who knew trying to model a society can be that hard?
EFG is defined by \((\mathcal{N}\cup\{c\},\mathcal{H},\mathcal{Z},\mathcal{A},\{\mathcal{R}^i\},\tau,\pi^c,\mathcal{S})\). Check §2.2.2.


Other game properties
MG and EFG (?) are the two dominant frameworks in modelling MARL; these two frameworks are both implemented as a MARL (and its corresponding learning algorithms). The rest of the game models are more niche with their own interesting theoretical properties that augments the aformentioned two: Stackelberg, Linear-quadratic (LQ), Morse-Smale, and Hamiltonian (requires further reading.)
















Mean-field games (MFG) and mean-field MARL
Source: Mean Field Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (2020)
The motivation for MARL is for some environment (e.g., market dynamics) the non-stationarity can be completely explained by other agents (e.g., market participants). However, this requires omdelling interactions between each agents pair-wise via joint-action space, resulting in non-polynomial (i.e., exponential \(|\mathcal{A}|^N\)) scaling for \(N\) many agents. MFG is an approximation that allows linear scaling at \(2|\mathcal{A}|\) by aggregating all external agents’ effects as an average term: \(\mu^i_t\). Basically, we would be training \(n\) single-agent RL with an extra \(\mu^i_t\) parameter for the Q-value to incorporate the overall effects from other agents in the environment.
Mean field approximation. Suppose the pairwise Q-value \(Q^j(s,a^j,a^k)\) is twice-differentiable w.r.t. action \(a^k\) where \(\mathbf{a}=\begin{bmatrix}a_1 & a_2 & \cdots & a_N \end{bmatrix}^\top\). Additionally, \(a^k=\mu^j+\delta a^{j,k}\) where \(\mu^j=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^n a^k\). \[
\begin{align}
Q^j(s,\mathbf{a})
&\triangleq \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^N Q^j (s, a^j, a^k) \\
&= \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^N \bigg(Q^j (s, a^j, \mu^j) + \nabla_{\mu^j}Q^j(s, a^j, \mu^j)\delta a^{j,k} + \frac{1}{2}\delta a^{j,k} \cdot \nabla^2_{a_*^{j,k}}(s,a^j,a_*^{j,k}) \cdot \delta a^{j,k}\bigg) \\
&= Q^j (s, a^j, \mu^j) +  \nabla_{\mu^j}Q^j(s, a^j, \mu^j) \bigg( \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^N \delta a^{j,k} \bigg) + \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^N R^j_{s,a^j}(a^k)  \\
&\approx Q^j (s, a^j, \mu^j) \\
\end{align}
\] In the first equality, we suppose the Q-function can be approximated via pair-wise factorization. In the second equality, we use 1st order Taylor series with Lagrange remainder \(R^j_{s,a^j}\) where \(\forall i, a_*^{j,k}[i] \in (\mu^j[i], a^k[i])\). At the third equality, the 1st-order term disappears due to \(\sum_k\delta a^{j,k}=0\). Finally, the second-order remainder term \(R\) can also be dropped since it’s usually many small fluctations near zero that cancels each correlation out between every \(a^j\) and \(a^k\) pair. However, this second-order remainder approximation remains to be seen if it holds in our case.
Nash equilibrium convergence.
Theorem 1 from the source proves the existence of convergence for MFG towards a mean field equilibrium, where every agents’ policy \(\pi^i\) is Nash-optimal. We won’t directly look at the proof since it requires concepts of filtration and filtered probability space for stochastic process (requires further reading.) However, we can look at what properties are required to satisfy the convergence:

Every action-value pair is visited infinitely often and the reward is bounded (i.e., no infinite rewards for a given state-action pair.)
Agent’s policy is GLIE (greedy at the limit with infinite exploration.)

Since we need every state-action pair to be visited infinitely often but also require the policy to be greedy eventually. Unless the greedy policy also visits every state-action pair, this condition would still, regardless, be satisfied asymptotically (\(t\to\infty\)).

At every time \(t\), a Nash equilibrium exists (as Nash-optimal policy \(\pi_*\)) as a global optimum or as a saddle point.

Empirically, this is not necessary for learning algorithms to converge.

Learning rate \(\alpha_t(x)\): How much to keep the old Q-value versus replacing it with the new Q-value from TD.

\(0\leq \alpha_t(x) \leq 1\)
\(\sum a_t(x)=\infty\)
\(\sum a_t^2(x)<\infty\)

An example that satisfies both constraints: Harmonic series.


Have finite states (e.g., no continuous states)

In practice, just have a good learner that explores all options that eventually takes a greedy policy. Popular function approximators like neural networks have self-regularizing properties that should be able to handle “continuous” states—digitally. And, don’t give a state-action pair infinite reward.
Additional directions of reading near-term:

partial observability (POMF): Partially Observable Mean Field Reinforcement Learning (2021)
applications in order book simulation and optimal execution: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning in a Realistic Limit Order Book Market Simulation (2020)
